schools.summary: Matthew Henry reads David's unease in a cedar house while the ark lies in curtains as holy ambition — 'What shall I do for God?' — and praises Nathan's readiness to encourage a good purpose.

Ellicott dates the events to David's later reign of rest (2 Samuel 7:1; 1 Chr 17:8), notes the Chronicler's fondness for the name 'David,' and identifies the cedar house as Hiram's palace of 1 Chr 14:1.
Clarke and Wesley lay out the chapter's shape and refer the whole to its twin at 2 Samuel 7, 'with little variation.'

themes: David's zeal to build for God
for thy servant's sake — Christ the Word and Servant

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch open the oracle through this verse of Nathan's approval; Ellicott weighs the Chronicles/Samuel variants ('Do' vs 'Go, do'; 'God' vs 'Jehovah') and glosses the heart as the seat of mind and will.

themes: the heart as seat of the will
a blessing awaiting the divine word

schools.summary: Ellicott reads 'the same night' as marking a dream-revelation (cf. Job 4:13); Keil and Delitzsch carry the oracle from Nathan's approval into the corrective night word.
Matthew Henry notes how God prevents the very good purpose he graciously accepts, redirecting both king and prophet.

Ellicott renders 'It is not thou that shalt build me the house,' the emphasis on the person, and notes the Chronicler's 'the house' looks back on Solomon's Temple; Keil and Delitzsch set the refusal as the hinge of a reversal.
Matthew Henry: David must prepare but not perform, as Moses saw Canaan and left it to Joshua — the 'No' spoken in love, not reproach.

themes: 'it is not thou that shalt build'

schools.summary: Ellicott finds the Hebrew compressed almost past sense ('from tent to tent, and from dwelling'), reconstructs the reading, and ties it to the old sanctuaries (Bethel, Mizpeh, Shiloh); mishkan is broader than 'tent.'
Clarke, following the Targum and Jarchi, traces the wandering tabernacle from Gilgal to Nob to Shiloh to Gibeon.

themes: God's long acceptance of a tent

schools.summary: Ellicott parses the rhetorical question phrase by phrase: 'wheresoever'='as long as'; 'judges' replaces Samuel's harder 'tribes'; 'to feed'=to shepherd, to govern.
Matthew Henry: God's ark was content with a tabernacle and never demanded a house — we may be content awhile with mean accommodations.
Ellicott stresses the emphatic pronoun ('I it was who took thee'), reads 'ruler' as nagid, and ties the sheepcote-call to Psalms 78:70-72.
Matthew Henry makes the verse the answer to a wounded thought: to be ruler over Israel is 'honour enough,' the temple left to one who comes after.

themes: humbled in order to be exalted

schools.summary: Ellicott reads 'cut off all thine enemies' of Saul's fall and David's wars, and takes 'made thee a name' as a still-opening promise — a name like the great sovereigns of Egypt, Babylon, Tyre, and the Hittites.
Matthew Henry: God gave David victories and a name and made him fit for war, leaving the house to one never cut out for a soldier.

themes: the retrospect bends toward the covenant

schools.summary: Ellicott compares the planting language to Exodus 15:17 and Psalms 44:2-3, notes the uncertain tribal boundaries down to David's day, and glosses 'children of wickedness' and the Aramaic 'waste them.'
Clarke reads 'neither shall the children of wickedness waste them' as a pledge that Israel shall not return to Egyptian servitude.

themes: a home for the everlasting house
rest pointing to David's greater Son

schools.summary: Ellicott savours the 'ironical inversion' — David would build God a house, and God will build David a house — with 'house' as offspring (Psalms 127:1) and the deliberate wordplay banah/banim, build/sons.
Wesley: since God's own act prevented the work, David is as fully recompensed as if it had been done.
Matthew Henry: 'The Lord will build thee a house, and annex the crown of Israel to it'; the willing mind is accepted and rewarded.

Keil and Delitzsch call 'to go with thy fathers' more primitive than Samuel's idiom and defend the wording against a charge of harmonizing; Ellicott holds the Chronicler paraphrased Samuel's 'from thy bowels' to suit a later age.

debates.question: Is the Chronicler's 'which shall be of thy sons' a deliberate alteration of Samuel's 'from thy bowels'?

sides.position: Yes — a paraphrase to suit a later age, or an alteration made to escape a difficulty
holders: Bertheau (as reported by Keil and Delitzsch)

sides.position: No — an independent, even more primitive form of the tradition, wrongly suspected of harmonizing tampering

themes: an established kingdom straining beyond Solomon

schools.summary: Ellicott marks the emphatic 'He' and the abridgment of Samuel's phrasing; Keil and Delitzsch let the verse stand as the second panel of the promise, the son doing what the father may not.
Clarke reads 'establish his throne for ever' as literally true only of Christ, of whom David was a type (Isaiah 9:7).

Ellicott hears the royal adoption of Psalms 2:7 and argues the omission of Samuel's 'if he commit iniquity' is deliberate — the prophecy looks beyond Solomon to Christ, to whom no warning of chastisement can apply.
Clarke reads 'I will not take my mercy away' as a pledge not to cut off David's house as Saul's was cut off.

themes: the omitted threat of the rod

schools.summary: Ellicott notes the change to 'mine house and my kingdom,' bringing out the theocratic nature of the throne — Solomon and his heirs as vicegerents of Jehovah; Keil and Delitzsch let the words climax Nathan's message.
Wesley: to be set in God's house and kingdom 'agrees but imperfectly with Solomon... strictly and properly with Christ,' king of all nations and head of the church of Jew and Gentile.
Matthew Henry: David must look past his family's temporal prosperity to the kingdom of the Messiah whose throne is established for evermore.

Ellicott reflects on the record itself: neither Samuel nor Chronicles gives a verbatim report but the essence of Nathan's words, so the two narratives each sometimes preserve the more original form; the prayer that follows 'breathes the genuine Davidic spirit.'

outliers.note: Grants that David's prayer may be an 'ideal soliloquy' rather than his verbatim words — a critical caution about the exactness of the report, though he affirms its genuine Davidic spirit.

schools.summary: Matthew Henry dwells on the prayer as a model of humble, believing devotion, and on the added acknowledgment 'according to the estate of a man of high degree,' with its further reading that 'points at the Messiah' — David seeing Christ's day, the Word made flesh (Psalms 110:1; Hebrews 2:6-7).

Ellicott sets the scene in the tent of the ark, weighs the divine names (Jehovah Elohim vs Samuel's Adonai Jehovah), and compares David's last words (2 Samuel 23:5); Keil and Delitzsch carry the prayer forward.
Clarke: 'I am not of any regal family, and have no natural right to the throne'; Wesley echoes Henry's wonder at David's humble, fervent prayer.

Ellicott calls the clause obscure and offers competing renderings of Samuel's 'torah of man' — 'like man's teaching that bringeth up' (revelation as moral discipline) or 'after the manner of men that exalteth' (as benefactors advance their favourites).
Matthew Henry reads the same phrase as a glimpse of the Messiah — David beholding 'according to the form of a man' the majesty of the Lord God.

debates.question: How should the obscure clause (Samuel's 'this is the law of man') be rendered?

sides.position: A lesson to mankind — God's revelation as fatherly teaching, or as the way great men advance those they favour
A prophetic sight of the Messiah, David beholding Christ 'in the form of a man'

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch let the verse open David's confession that words have run out; Ellicott works the difficult Hebrew, suspecting 'for the honour' a corruption of 'to speak' and 'of thy servant' a mistaken repetition.
Wesley: the honour God puts on his servants by taking them into covenant is so great they cannot desire to be more highly honoured.
Matthew Henry: believers could not, if they sat down to wish, speak more for their own honour than God's word has already spoken over them.

Ellicott judges Samuel's 'for thy word's sake' the more original, calls 'greatness... greatnesses' a scribe's error, and notes the miracle of prediction as the mark of the true God over idols (Isaiah 41:21).
Wesley records the variant, that Samuel reads 'for thy word's sake,' for the sake of the promise.
Matthew Henry finds a treasure in the difference: Christ being both the Word (Revelation 19:13) and the Servant (Isaiah 42:1) of God, it is for his sake the promises are yea and amen.

debates.question: Is the change from Samuel's 'for thy word's sake' to 'for thy servant's sake' textual or theological?

sides.position: A textual shift; Samuel's 'for thy word's sake' is the more original reading
A providential doubling revealing the one Christ, who is both the Word and the Servant of God

schools.summary: Ellicott notes the Chronicler's compression against the fuller confession of 2 Samuel 7:22; Keil and Delitzsch let the verse stand as the theological centre of David's praise — from a particular mercy to the sole deity of the LORD, grounded in what Israel 'heard with our ears.'

Ellicott renders 'a single, isolated race' (Numbers 23:9), distinguishes goy from am, and notes the Chronicler omits Samuel's 'and his gods,' treating heathen deities as non-entities; Keil and Delitzsch weigh the tight construction of 'great and fearful deeds.'

Ellicott renders the two-directional covenant bond ('thou gavest Israel unto thyself... becamest unto them for a God') and anchors it in the formula from Genesis 17:7-8 and Exodus 6:7; Keil and Delitzsch make it the ground of the petitions that follow.

themes: the national 'for ever' underwrites the dynastic

schools.summary: Ellicott parses the petition — 'let the word be upheld,' 'do as thou hast said' — as asking only that God be true to his own pledge; Keil and Delitzsch set it at the head of the prayer's closing plea.

themes: the plea as good as granted

schools.summary: Ellicott notes the emphatic 'let it even be established' (absent in Samuel) and the confession 'Jehovah Sabaoth, God of Israel, is God to Israel,' the closing clause possibly future praise of the established house.
Wesley draws out 'a God to Israel' — God is really to his people what he has styled himself, answering the title in care and mercy.
Matthew Henry distinguishes the God 'of' Israel (bearing the name) from a God 'to' Israel (filling the relation), unlike the empty gods of Assyria, Egypt, Hamath, and Arpad.

themes: God of Israel vs a God to Israel
the dynasty as proof of the name

schools.summary: Ellicott renders 'hast told' as 'uncovered the ear' (1 Samuel 9:15) and 'found in his heart' as 'hath found his heart' — hath taken courage, a unique Hebrew phrase.
Clarke, from the Targum, gives 'hath found an opening of mouth, that he might pray' — the revelation unstops the servant's lips.

themes: prayer as the echo of promise
boldness born of the word heard

schools.summary: Ellicott renders 'Jehovah, thou art the true God' and notes the Chronicler omits Samuel's 'and thy words become truth'; Keil and Delitzsch make the verse the pivot between plea and expected blessing — deity and promise together making the outcome certain.

themes: Godhead and faithfulness as one guarantee

schools.summary: Ellicott sharpens the closing Hebrew — 'thou hast willed to bless,' 'for thou, Jehovah, hast blessed' — a past act on which the future rests, the blessing irreversible like Balaam's word (Numbers 22:6).
Clarke (from the Targum) reads 'thou beginnest to bless... therefore blessed for ever'; Wesley marks that the prayer, like the promise, ends on 'for ever.'
Matthew Henry hears the language of triumphant faith: Samuel's holy desire becomes Chronicles' holy assurance — 'thou blessest, and it shall be blessed for ever.'

themes: the everlasting house of David's Son
